answers.intake_q1: When did your back pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly your back pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury or trauma to your back or ribs?
answers.intake_q4: Any fever, chills, or night sweats since the pain started?
answers.intake_q5: Any shortness of breath, cough, or chest pain?
answers.intake_q6: Any numbness, weakness, or tingling in your legs or feet?
answers.intake_q7: Have you noticed any swelling, bruising, or tenderness over the painful area?
answers.intake_q8: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your back pain so far?
answers.intake_a8: I tried tylenol but it didn't help very much
answers.intake_q9: Any recent weight loss, night pain, or history of cancer?
answers.intake_q10: Any history of osteoporosis or recent steroid use?

note.soap:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with left side lower rib cage back pain that started 3 weeks ago. She denies any recent injury or trauma, fever, chills, night sweats, shortness of breath, cough, chest pain, numbness, weakness, or tingling in her legs or feet. She reports tenderness over the painful area. She has tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) with minimal relief. There is no history of recent weight loss, night pain, cancer, osteoporosis, or steroid use.

O: Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is musculoskeletal pain localized to the left lower rib cage area, likely due to a mechanical cause given the absence of systemic symptoms or neurological deficits.
Differential:
1. Musculoskeletal strain or sprain
2. Costochondritis or rib inflammation
3. Intercostal muscle strain
4. Early presentation of a visceral cause such as pleuritis or referred pain from abdominal organs

P: 
- Acetaminophen 500–1000 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for pain; max 3 grams/day (adjust for liver disease).
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated.
 - Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. (avoid in renal disease; especially severe CKD/dialysis)
- Medication safety considerations: Avoid NSAIDs if there is a history of renal disease, liver disease, or gastrointestinal bleeding; monitor for side effects.
- Return precautions: Advise patient to seek care if she develops fever, worsening pain, neurological symptoms (numbness, weakness), chest pain, shortness of breath, or if pain persists beyond 6 weeks.
- Testing: No immediate testing indicated given absence of red flags; consider imaging if symptoms worsen or fail to improve with conservative treatment.

evidence.title: NIAMS: Living With Back Pain (patient-friendly NIH resource)
evidence.snippet: Acute back pain happens suddenly and usually lasts a few days to a few weeks. Back pain that lasts longer than 12 weeks is called chronic back pain.   Who Gets Back Pain? Image Anyone can have back pain. You may be more likely to have back pain because of the following: Age: Back pain is more common as you age. Fitness level: Back pain is more common if…

evidence.title: Back Injuries | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: extending from your neck to your pelvis. Back injuries can result from sports injuries, work around the house or in the garden, or a sudden jolt such as a car accident. evidence.title: AAFP Choosing Wisely: Imaging for Low Back Pain (Red Flags)
evidence.snippet: Information Low back pain is one of the most common reasons for an outpatient visit.
